section.title: Daniel in the Lions' Den
section.sectionRationale: Under a new empire, jealous officials trap the aged Daniel with a law against praying to anyone but the king. Daniel keeps praying openly toward Jerusalem, is thrown to the lions, and is delivered by God, who shuts the lions' mouths. The last court narrative crowns the theme of the first six chapters: God preserves His faithful servant in the face of death, and the pagan king is led to praise the living God whose kingdom never ends.
section.thesis: When loyalty to God is criminalized, Daniel prays as he always has, trusting God with the cost; God sends His angel to shut the lions' mouths, vindicating His innocent servant, so that the king proclaims the living God whose dominion will never end.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the plot and the trap of an unchangeable law; Daniel keeps praying openly; cast into the den under the king's seal; the lions' mouths shut and Daniel brought up unharmed; and Darius decrees praise to the living God.

units.label: The plot and the trap
units.note: a law that cannot be changed

verses.text: Now it pleased Darius to appoint 120 satraps to rule throughout the kingdom,
and over them three administrators, including Daniel, to whom these satraps were accountable so that the king would not suffer loss.
Soon, by his extraordinary spirit, Daniel distinguished himself among the administrators and satraps. So the king planned to set him over the whole kingdom.

Thus the administrators and satraps sought a charge against Daniel concerning the kingdom, but they could find no charge or corruption, because he was trustworthy, and no negligence or corruption was found in him.
Finally these men said, “We will never find any charge against this Daniel unless we find something against him concerning the law of his God.”
So the administrators and satraps went together to the king and said, “O King Darius, may you live forever!

All the royal administrators, prefects, satraps, advisers, and governors have agreed that the king should establish an ordinance and enforce a decree that for thirty days anyone who petitions any god or man except you, O king, will be thrown into the den of lions.
Therefore, O king, establish the decree and sign the document so that it cannot be changed, in accordance with the {{dath|law}} of the Medes and Persians, which cannot be repealed.”
Therefore King Darius signed the written decree.

units.label: Daniel keeps praying openly
units.note: just as he had done before

verses.text: Now when Daniel learned that the document had been signed, he went into his house, where the windows of his upper room opened toward Jerusalem, and three times a day he got down on his knees, {{tsela|prayed}}, and gave thanks to his God, just as he had done before.
Then these men went as a group and found Daniel petitioning and imploring his God.

So they approached the king and asked about his royal decree: “Did you not sign a decree that for thirty days any man who petitions any god or man except you, O king, will be thrown into the den of lions?” The king replied, “According to the law of the Medes and Persians the order stands, and it cannot be repealed.”
Then they told the king, “Daniel, one of the exiles from Judah, shows no regard for you, O king, or for the decree that you have signed. He still makes his petition three times a day.”

units.label: Cast into the den under the king's seal

verses.text: As soon as the king heard this, he was deeply distressed and set his mind on delivering Daniel, and he labored until sundown to rescue him.
Then the men approached the king together and said to him, “Remember, O king, that by the law of the Medes and Persians no decree or ordinance established by the king can be changed.”
So the king gave the order, and they brought Daniel and threw him into the den of lions. The king said to Daniel, “May your God, whom you serve continually, deliver you!”
A stone was brought and placed over the mouth of the den, and the king sealed it with his own signet ring and with the rings of his nobles, so that nothing concerning Daniel could be changed.
Then the king went to his palace and spent the night fasting. No entertainment was brought before him, and sleep fled from him.
At the first light of dawn, the king got up and hurried to the den of lions.
When he reached the den, he cried out in a voice of anguish, “O Daniel, servant of the {{elaha_chayya|living God}}, has your God, whom you serve continually, been able to deliver you from the lions?”
Then Daniel replied, “O king, may you live forever!
My God sent His angel and shut the mouths of the lions. They have not hurt me, for I was found innocent in His sight, and I have done no wrong against you, O king.”
The king was overjoyed and gave orders to lift Daniel out of the den, and when Daniel was lifted out of the den, no wounds whatsoever were found on him, because he had trusted in his God.

At the command of the king, the men who had falsely accused Daniel were brought and thrown into the den of lions, they and their children and wives. And before they had reached the bottom of the den, the lions overpowered them and crushed all their bones.
Then King Darius wrote to the people of every nation and language throughout the land: “May your prosperity abound.

I hereby decree that in every part of my kingdom, men are to tremble in fear before the God of Daniel: For He is the living God, and He endures forever; His kingdom will never be destroyed, and His dominion will never end.
He {{shezib|delivers and rescues}}; He performs signs and wonders in the heavens and on the earth, for He has rescued Daniel from the power of the lions.”
So Daniel prospered during the reign of Darius and the reign of Cyrus the Persian.

dath.gloss: A law, decree, or edict. The plot hinges on the dath of the Medes and Persians, a royal law that even the king cannot revoke. The unchangeable human decree is set against the higher law and living God to whom Daniel is loyal; the rigid edict traps the king but cannot bind God.

tsela.gloss: To pray, to bow in prayer. When the ban is signed, Daniel simply keeps on praying toward Jerusalem three times a day, just as before. His prayer is not defiance staged for show but steady, humble devotion that will not be interrupted by a royal threat.

elaha_chayya.gloss: The living God. Both the anxious king and the closing decree call the LORD the living God, in pointed contrast to the dead idols of gold and stone. He is the God who acts, who hears prayer, who shuts lions' mouths, and who endures forever while empires rise and fall.

shezib.gloss: To rescue, deliver, save. The same verb sounded through the fiery furnace now rings through the lions' den: God delivers and rescues, and He has rescued Daniel from the power of the lions. It is the signature act of the living God, who saves His trusting servants.

basic.context: Under Darius, Daniel rises to the top of the government, and jealous rivals look for a way to destroy him. Finding him blameless, they trick the king into a thirty-day law that anyone who prays to any god or man but the king will be thrown to the lions. Daniel keeps praying openly toward Jerusalem, is caught, and is thrown into the den. God sends His angel to shut the lions' mouths, Daniel is found unharmed, and the king proclaims that the living God, who rescued Daniel, endures forever.
basic.hermeneutics: This famous story is the climax of the court narratives and a mirror of the fiery furnace. Daniel does not flaunt his prayer or provoke a fight; he simply refuses to stop worshiping God when worship is outlawed. His obedience shows the limit of loyalty to the state: we honor rulers, but we cannot obey a command to abandon God. God's deliverance is real, though as elsewhere in Scripture, faithful people are not always spared.
basic.originalLanguages: The trap is the dath, the unchangeable law of the Medes and Persians. Against it Daniel keeps up his tsela, his steady prayer. Both the frightened king and his final decree name the LORD the elaha chayya, the living God, over against dead idols; and the God who shezib, delivers and rescues, shuts the lions' mouths.
basic.typology: Daniel, innocent yet condemned, sealed in a pit under a stone, and brought out alive at dawn, is a striking picture of Christ, who was falsely accused, sealed in a tomb, and raised on the third day. The shut mouths of the lions foreshadow the defeat of death itself in the resurrection.
basic.christ: The innocent man cast into the pit and raised up unharmed points to Jesus, condemned though guiltless, laid in a sealed tomb, and raised to life. As God delivered Daniel from the lions, so God delivered Christ from death, and in Him delivers all who trust Him from the mouth of the grave. Christ is the living God who rescues His people.
basic.god: God is the living God who hears prayer and acts, who is stronger than lions, kings, and irreversible laws. He honors the faith of those who trust Him, sends His angel to protect them, and rules a kingdom that, unlike every human empire in this book, will never be destroyed. He is worthy of a worship no law can forbid.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 91 promises God's protection for those who trust Him. Acts 5 records the apostles saying we must obey God rather than men. And Hebrews 11 lists those who through faith shut the mouths of lions, with Daniel in view.
basic.oneStory: The last of Babylon's kings gives way to Persia, but God's servant is preserved and God's name is honored again by a pagan throne. The living God who rescues Daniel from the lions is the God who will rescue His people from death itself in Christ, and whose everlasting kingdom stands when all the empires of Daniel have crumbled to dust.

context.literary: The sixth and final court narrative, closely paralleling chapter 3: a royal decree, a faithful refusal, a death sentence carried out, a miraculous deliverance, and a pagan king's praise. It caps the first half of the book and forms a bridge to the visions, which open in the next chapter with Daniel himself as the seer.
context.historical: The law of the Medes and Persians was famed for being unalterable once issued, which is why even the king cannot save Daniel. Lions were kept by ancient kings, and execution by wild beasts is well attested. Daniel is now an old man, having served through the whole Babylonian era and into the Persian.
context.canonical: The story stands with the fiery furnace as a paradigm of faithful witness under persecution, echoed when the apostles declare they must obey God rather than men. Its imagery of an innocent man sealed in a pit and raised at dawn feeds Christian reflection on the death and resurrection of Christ, and Hebrews 11 explicitly recalls those who shut the mouths of lions.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the living God preserves and vindicates His faithful servant even when loyalty to Him is made a crime, and to call God's people to unbroken worship and rulers to acknowledge the God whose kingdom never ends.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a particular deliverance; it does not promise that every believer thrown to the lions will be spared, as the martyrs remind us. What it prescribes is Daniel's steady faithfulness, his refusal to stop worshiping God, and his trust in God with the outcome, whatever it may be.

debates.question: Does Daniel's example justify disobeying the government?

views.label: Obedience is the rule; disobedience the narrow exception
views.case: Scripture calls believers to honor and submit to governing authorities, Romans 13; Daniel served the empire loyally and blamelessly, and disobeyed only the single command that forced him to stop worshiping God, showing that civil disobedience is limited to cases where the state commands what God forbids or forbids what God commands.

views.label: Faithful witness may accept the penalty rather than hide
views.case: Daniel does not flee, rebel, or even conceal his prayer; he keeps worshiping openly and is willing to bear the legal consequence, modeling a conscientious, non-violent refusal that submits to punishment while refusing to sin, as the apostles later do in Acts 5:29.

debates.bearing: Both views agree that loyalty to God is supreme and honor to rulers is real; the believer obeys the state in all things except where obedience would mean disobeying God, and then accepts the cost with a clear conscience.

study.originalLanguages: The whole crisis is built on the dath, the law of the Medes and Persians, a decree so binding that not even the king who signed it can undo it. Against that immovable human law stands Daniel's quiet tsela, his prayer three times a day toward Jerusalem, unchanged by the threat. The pagan king, pacing through a sleepless night, calls the LORD the elaha chayya, the living God, and so does his final decree, contrasting Him with the lifeless idols the book has mocked. And the climactic word is shezib, to rescue: the living God delivers and rescues, and He has rescued Daniel from the power of the lions.

typology.type: The innocent man condemned, sealed in a pit under a stone
typology.fulfillment: Christ, falsely accused and laid in a sealed tomb, John 19:38-42 and Matthew 27:66.

typology.type: Brought up alive at dawn, unharmed
typology.fulfillment: The resurrection of Christ, raised on the third day, death unable to hold Him, Acts 2:24.

typology.type: The lions' mouths shut by God's angel
typology.fulfillment: The defeat of death and the devil, who prowls like a lion but is overcome in Christ, 1 Peter 5:8 and Hebrews 2:14-15.

study.christ: Daniel, blameless yet condemned, cast into a pit sealed with a stone and the king's own ring, and lifted out alive at the first light of dawn, is a vivid foreshadowing of Jesus. Christ was falsely accused though wholly innocent, sealed in a tomb, and raised on the third day, and no wound of death could hold Him. As God shut the lions' mouths and delivered His servant, so God overcame the grave and delivered His Son, and in Him rescues all who trust Him from the jaws of death. The living God who rescued Daniel is the God who raises the dead.
study.god: God is the living God, in sharp contrast to the dead idols the book has ridiculed; He hears the prayers of His servants, sends His angel to protect them, and is mightier than lions, kings, and unbreakable laws. He honors those who trust Him and vindicates the innocent, and He rules a kingdom that will never be destroyed while every empire in Daniel passes away. He is worthy of a worship that no decree of men can forbid.

crossReferences.note: We must obey God rather than men, the principle enacted in Daniel's steady prayer.
God commands His angels to guard the one who trusts Him, who treads on the lion, echoed in the den.
By faith the saints shut the mouths of lions, with Daniel in view among the heroes of faith.
The devil prowls like a roaring lion, whose power is broken for those who resist him firm in faith.

study.oneStory: As Babylon gives way to Persia, God's aged servant is preserved in the lions' den and God's name is again honored from a pagan throne. The living God who shuts the lions' mouths and rescues Daniel is the God who will rescue His people from death itself in Christ, the innocent one sealed in a tomb and raised at dawn. When every empire charted in Daniel has crumbled, the kingdom of this living God still stands, for His dominion will never end.

quiz.q: Why could Daniel's enemies find no ordinary charge against him?

options.text: He was trustworthy and blameless, with no corruption or negligence in his work.
options.note: Daniel 6:4 stresses his integrity; only his faith could be used against him.

options.text: He bribed the officials to stay silent.
options.note: The opposite; he was incorruptible, which is why they found nothing.

options.text: He hid his misdeeds carefully.
options.note: There were no misdeeds to hide; he was found blameless.

options.text: The king protected him from all scrutiny.
options.note: They investigated him thoroughly and still found nothing.

quiz.q: How did Daniel respond when the decree against prayer was signed?

options.text: He kept praying toward Jerusalem three times a day, just as he always had.
options.note: Daniel 6:10 shows steady, unbroken devotion, not a staged protest.

options.text: He stopped praying for thirty days to stay safe.
options.note: He did not stop; he continued praying as before.

options.text: He prayed secretly where no one could see.
options.note: He prayed openly at his window, just as he had done before.

options.text: He fled the kingdom to avoid the law.
options.note: He stayed and kept worshiping, ready to bear the cost.

quiz.q: How was Daniel delivered in the den?

options.text: God sent His angel and shut the mouths of the lions, and Daniel was unharmed.
options.note: Daniel 6:22 credits God's angel with shutting the lions' mouths.

options.text: The lions had already been fed and were not hungry.
options.note: The text attributes the rescue to God's angel, not to well-fed lions.

options.text: Daniel fought off the lions with his hands.
options.note: He did nothing but trust God, who shut the lions' mouths.

options.text: The king pulled him out before the lions arrived.
options.note: Daniel spent the night among the lions, kept safe by God.

quiz.q: What did King Darius decree after Daniel's deliverance?

options.text: That people everywhere should fear the living God of Daniel, whose kingdom will never end.
options.note: Daniel 6:26-27 records his praise of the living, eternal God.

options.text: That Daniel should be executed after all.
options.note: The opposite; he honored Daniel and praised his God.

options.text: That prayer to any god should be banned permanently.
options.note: He reversed course, commanding reverence for the God of Daniel.

options.text: That the lions should be worshiped.
options.note: He directed worship to the living God who rescued Daniel.

quiz.q: How does Daniel in the den point us to Christ?

options.text: An innocent man sealed in a pit under a stone and brought out alive at dawn foreshadows Christ's death and resurrection.
options.note: The pattern anticipates the sealed tomb and the risen Lord, Matthew 27:66 and Acts 2:24.

options.text: It teaches that faithful people are always kept from physical harm.
options.note: God did deliver Daniel, but the deeper pointer is to Christ's resurrection, not a guarantee of safety.

options.text: It shows that lions can be tamed by brave men.
options.note: The rescue was God's act; the point is God's deliverance, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The innocent one sealed in a pit and raised at dawn clearly foreshadows Jesus.

questions: Daniel's enemies could find nothing against him except his faith. If people searched your life for a charge, would only your devotion to God stand out?
When prayer was outlawed, Daniel simply kept doing what he had always done. How settled and regular is your own life of prayer, before any pressure comes?
Daniel honored the king yet would not obey a law that forbade worshiping God. Where might you face a choice between pleasing people and staying faithful to God?
Daniel trusted God with the outcome, whether he lived or died. How does the promise of resurrection free you to obey God even when it is costly?
A pagan king ended up praising the living God because of one faithful life. How might your quiet faithfulness become a witness to those who hold power over you?

translationNotes.note: The law of the Medes and Persians was famed as unalterable once issued, which is why even the king cannot rescind the decree that traps Daniel.
Praying toward Jerusalem, three times a day, reflects the practice of facing the temple, as in Solomon's prayer, 1 Kings 8:44-48.
God sent His angel, the same language as the fiery furnace, marks the deliverance as God's direct act on behalf of His innocent servant.
The final line may be read as the reign of Darius and the reign of Cyrus, or as the reign of Darius, that is, the reign of Cyrus, bearing on the identity of Darius the Mede.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Daniel 6:1-28. Em dashes in verses 8 and 24 converted to commas. Poetic lines in verses 26 to 27 rendered as flowing prose. Chapter 6 is Aramaic, so load-bearing lexicon entries use Aramaic. No em or en dashes used.
